---
title: "Background and landscape"
description: "The threat model, the adversary in the abstract, the economics of automation, and the commercial vendor landscape — read as evidence about the territory, not as vendor evaluation."
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: false
---

::: {.callout-warning}
## Draft — review-pending dependencies

This page is a **first prose draft**, not finished site content. It rests on register sources that are currently either `migrated — review pending` or `needs review` (`evidence-register.qmd`). The sources added since the first draft (SRC-027 onward) are largely `needs review` and were extracted by assistant agents (ChatGPT/GPT-5.5, Codex/GPT-5, Claude) **without** the per-source `working/register-entries/` files having been reviewed against the originals — so they are no more citable than the migrated rows, and arguably less. Every vendor figure and specific claim below carries its source id and, where it is a vendor-measured number, a verify-before-publish marker (⚠). The page now spans the SRC-001…072 corpus but **cites selectively**: foundations-layer sources (MDN HTTP references, PortSwigger authentication labs, browser-fingerprinting surveys) belong in Foundations/Techniques, not here, and are deliberately not pulled into the landscape narrative.

Nothing here is citable until the matching entry files are reviewed. In particular: the **full OWASP Automated Threat Handbook** is now extracted as SRC-027, but its entry was produced without access to the repo scope docs and is flagged `needs review` — so the OAT spine below should still be treated as anchored to OWASP's public project page (SRC-001) until SRC-027 is checked.

The review-pending dependency ledger at the foot of the page lists, per section, exactly what needs checking.
:::

## What this section claims

- The territory has a public, stable vocabulary for the *threats* (OWASP's OAT ontology) but not for the *defences* — the defence side is described almost entirely in vendor language.
- The adversary is best described by **capability gradient** and **motivation**, not by identity. Naming actors is out of scope; the sophistication taxonomy is the load-bearing structure.
- The "economics of bot activity" the project can speak to honestly is the **cost structure of automation** (tooling, proxies, solvers), not the criminal money-flow economics — that boundary is a deliberate scope decision and is flagged below.
- The commercial vendor landscape is read as **evidence about what the field claims to do**, never as a comparison or ranking. Most quantitative claims in this area are vendor-measured and self-reported; the page marks them as such.
- The threat model is **shifting** with browser-native automation and AI agents, and the older bot-detection framing addresses this poorly.
- Across the whole corpus, the evidence is **far stronger on capability and market existence than on real-world prevalence or efficacy**. The register now classifies every source on an `operational proximity` axis (`capability` → `claimed` → `observed` → `measured`); the corpus concentrates at `capability`/`claimed`, `observed` is almost entirely vendor-measured telemetry, and `measured` is a handful of narrow studies. This is the single most important thing to hold in mind when reading anything below.

## How to read this section

Two editorial commitments shape everything here, both inherited from the project's working principles.

**Vendors are evidence, not subjects.** Marketing pages, white papers, and threat reports tell us what the field *says it does*. They are cited as signal about the territory. They are never used to characterise, rank, or evaluate an individual company. A sentence like "DataDome frames detection around intent" is a claim about the field's vocabulary; it is not an endorsement, and it is not a measurement of whether intent-based detection works.

**Methods before actors.** A technique described in the abstract is in scope. A campaign attributed to a named group is not. This is a sharp line and it is easy to cross when reading threat-intelligence material, most of which is organised around actors. Where a source's value is buried inside actor attribution, the project extracts the technique and discards the attribution.

There is also a scope interaction worth surfacing explicitly, because the two governing documents do not align perfectly. `PROJECT.md` §5 lists "the economics of bot activity" as part of this section. `EVIDENCE-REVIEW.md` §3 marks "the criminal economics of abuse — who makes money, how money flows" as *out* of scope. The reconciliation taken here: the **cost structure of automation** (what it costs an adversary to run bots — tooling, proxies, CAPTCHA-solving) is in scope, because it bears directly on what defences are trying to change. The **revenue structure of abuse** (who profits and how money moves) is out. This is a judgement, not a settled decision — flagged for review.

## The threat model

The underlying problem is constant across every abuse type the project covers: **automated activity at scale, directed at endpoints and flows built for human users, to extract value or cause harm**. The web or mobile interface is the attack surface; the automation is the adversary; the asset is whatever the flow protects — inventory, credentials, content, ad budget, account state.

The field's nearest thing to a shared vocabulary for this is the **OWASP Automated Threats to Web Applications** project, which the project adopts as the spine for threat-type naming [SRC-001, evidence basis: `taxonomy`]. The full **Automated Threat Handbook v1.3** is now extracted [SRC-027, `taxonomy`], giving all 21 OAT categories plus the handbook's countermeasure classes — but its entry is `needs review` and was produced without the repo scope docs, so codes are still confirmed against OWASP's public materials below rather than treated as settled. A few properties of OAT matter for how it is used here:

- It defines a fixed set of automated-threat *events* (currently 21, codes OAT-001 to OAT-021). The handbook gives each a description, the sectors it targets, the parties affected, and cross-mappings to CAPEC and WASC.
- It is explicitly an **ontology, not a ranked list**. The identification numbers were assigned arbitrarily, which is why the events are usually written alphabetically — there is no implied ordering or severity. This is a useful corrective to the instinct to read any security list as a Top-N.
- It names *abuse of intended functionality by automated means*, not implementation vulnerabilities. This is the right frame for the project: the flows under attack are working as designed; the problem is that they were designed for humans.

The abuse types in the project's scope map onto OAT cleanly, which is part of why OAT is the spine. The mapping below uses the codes confirmed against OWASP's public materials; codes not yet verified against the full handbook are named without a number and flagged.

- **Credential abuse.** Credential stuffing — replaying breached username/password pairs against a login (OAT-008) — and credential cracking — brute-forcing or guessing credentials against the auth flow (OAT-007). The distinction matters: stuffing tests *known* pairs, cracking searches a space. Both terminate in account takeover. This is now one of the better-evidenced areas: an end-to-end vendor-measured account of the credential-stuffing landscape [SRC-034 F5, `threat-intel`/`empirical-operational`, observed (vendor-measured)] ⚠ and an independent honey-identity measurement of leaked-credential use [SRC-048 Wardle, `empirical-academic`, measured] sit alongside the OAT mapping — though the F5 telemetry is 2020-era tooling and Wardle's experiment is small, dated, and paste-site-only.
- **Account creation.** Mass creation of fake accounts (OAT-019), as the entry point for spam, fraud, and abuse that wears the appearance of a different "user" each time.
- **Scraping.** Bulk extraction of content or data for use elsewhere (OAT-011) — including, increasingly, for LLM training and retrieval, which OWASP's own scraping entry now lists among the scraper types.
- **Scarcity and scalping.** Acquiring limited inventory ahead of legitimate buyers (scalping, OAT-005) and depleting it without ever purchasing (denial of inventory, OAT-021). The two differ in whether the goods are actually acquired. This is the one OAT area where the register now carries evidence beyond taxonomy: a public-sector appointment-abuse account [SRC-035 DVSA, `threat-intel`, observed platform-side] and a US enforcement record [SRC-050 FTC BOTS Act cases, `legal-record`, observed enforcement] document automated monitoring, holding, and resale of scarce slots/tickets — cited as platform-reported behaviour and FTC allegations/orders respectively, not as prevalence.
- **Ad and click fraud.** Falsifying impressions or clicks to drain or siphon ad spend (ad fraud, OAT-003).
- **Payment-flow abuse.** Carding and card-cracking — validating or brute-forcing stolen card data against payment forms — sit here; the specific OAT codes are not yet verified against the full handbook ⚠. A vendor-measured snapshot now lists payment-flow/carding abuse among observed bot activity [SRC-039 Thales/Imperva, `empirical-operational`, observed (vendor-measured)] ⚠.
- **Reconnaissance.** Footprinting (OAT-018) and related probing that precedes the rest.
- **Loyalty, promotion, and stored-value abuse**, and **API / business-logic abuse**, which OWASP treats partly through the events above and partly as cross-cutting; the precise OAT mapping for these is to be confirmed against the handbook ⚠.

::: {.callout-note}
## Worked example: the booking-style system

Throughout the project a mid-sized company running a **booking-style transactional system** — ticketing, reservations, appointment scheduling — anchors the abstract claims. It has the properties that make bot pressure an operational problem rather than a theoretical one: limited inventory or capacity, transactions with real value, a web/mobile interface as the primary surface, and limited in-house security capacity. Most of the OAT events above have a concrete reading against this system: scalping and denial of inventory against limited slots, credential stuffing against the account layer, scraping of availability and pricing, API abuse against the booking endpoints. The example is illustrative, not constitutive — the analysis aims to be general — but it keeps the threat model grounded. Two register sources now give it real-world instances rather than hypotheticals: the DVSA's account of automated booking and reselling of driving-test appointments [SRC-035] is a near-exact match for the worked example in a public-sector setting, and the FTC's first BOTS Act cases [SRC-050] document the same pattern against event ticketing. Both are platform/enforcement reports, not measurements, so they evidence that the pattern occurs — not how prevalent it is.
:::

## The adversary, in the abstract

Because naming actors is out of scope, the adversary is described by two axes that *are* in scope: **what they want** and **how capable they are**.

**Motivation** sorts roughly into resale (scalping, scraping for competitive or commercial data), monetisation of compromised assets (credential stuffing → account takeover → cashing out), fraud against value flows (ad fraud, carding, promotion abuse), and extraction for downstream use (content and data scraping, including for AI training). These are not exclusive, and the same tooling serves several.

**Capability** is the more analytically useful axis, and the project anchors it to the sophistication gradient from the Iliou work [SRC-011, evidence basis: `empirical-academic`], the single genuinely controlled academic anchor in the register. The gradient runs from simple bots (basic HTTP scripts, no browser, easily separated on request-level signals) through moderate (real browser engines, some evasion) to advanced (full browser automation with stealth layering, behavioural mimicry, and adaptation under pressure). The important and uncomfortable finding the project leans on is that **detection metrics measured against simple bots hide how weak detection is against advanced ones** — the Iliou 2019 result reports advanced-bot AUC around 0.68 at low false-positive rates [SRC-012, `empirical-academic`], which is the honest figure behind headline numbers that look much better. This gradient, not actor identity, is the structure the rest of the project hangs detection claims on. A more recent academic review corroborates the signal-family/evasion-class structure of this gradient [SRC-047 Martínez Llamas et al., `review`/`methods-taxonomy`], though as a review it adds taxonomy, not new measurement.

The gradient does not cleanly accommodate the newest adversary class: **AI browser agents** acting inside real, instrumented browsers. These are neither "simple HTTP script" nor "stealth-layered headless browser" — they are genuine browsers driven by a model, and the one independent measurement in the register finds they are nonetheless distinguishable from humans on browser and behavioural fingerprints in a controlled setting [SRC-033 FP-Agent, Wang et al., `empirical-academic`/`empirical-operational`, measured (honey-site)]. That is a benign-task, closed-world, point-in-time result against known agents, not evidence about adversarial humanisation or production prevalence — but it is the cleanest non-vendor anchor the project has for where AI agents sit relative to the gradient.

At the landscape level, the adversary's **tooling** forms a stack: a base automation layer (Playwright, Puppeteer, Selenium) [SRC-021, `capability-doc`]; evasion layers added on top (undetected-chromedriver, puppeteer-extra-plugin-stealth) [SRC-022, SRC-023, `tooling-readme`]; browser-like HTTP-client impersonation at the network level [SRC-029 Rnet, `capability-doc`]; and, increasingly, managed infrastructure that abstracts the whole stack — anti-scraping APIs, residential proxy pools, CAPTCHA-solving, and cloud browsers [SRC-024, SRC-025, SRC-026, SRC-053]. The register now also documents the public *mental model* for bypassing named commercial defenders as multi-layer signal alignment across IP, TLS, HTTP, fingerprint, session, and behaviour [SRC-045 ScrapingBee/PerimeterX-HUMAN, SRC-046 niespodd anti-detection map] — though these are scraper-side claims, not verified bypass success, and are high dual-use, so they are cited at technique-family level only. The mechanism of each — what signal each layer adds or hides — belongs in the Techniques section; what matters here is that the capability gradient is now substantially a function of *which tooling tier* an adversary buys into, not of bespoke skill.

## The economics of automation

The economics the project can speak to honestly is the **cost the adversary faces**, because that is what defences are trying to move. The clearest framing in the register is the attacker-cost model: defences that raise the per-request or per-success cost of automation — challenges, proof-of-execution, fingerprint friction — aim to make abuse uneconomic rather than impossible [SRC-008 Arkose, SRC-009 Kasada; evidence basis: `vendor-claim` / `threat-intel`]. Kasada's material in particular puts concrete prices on the inputs to an attack — solver services, proxy bandwidth, CAPTCHA-solving — as evidence of how cheap and commoditised the supply side has become ⚠ (these pricing claims are vendor-reported and not independently audited) [SRC-009].

The supply side is corroborated, in a softer sense, by the existence and positioning of the managed-tooling vendors themselves: anti-scraping-as-a-service, residential proxy networks, and cloud-browser platforms are sold openly with compliance-framed marketing [SRC-024 ScrapFly, SRC-025 Bright Data, `capability-doc` / `vendor-claim`]. The register now widens this picture: scraping is packaged as a routine commercial workflow with anti-bot handling sold as a core buying criterion [SRC-053 ScrapingBee web-scraping API, SRC-044 price-scraping tools comparison, `capability-doc`], and the scraper-side anti-detection ecosystem is mapped as a tooling market in its own right [SRC-046 niespodd]. A vendor-measured snapshot also reports proxy and solver use as standard inputs in the current "agentic age" of bot activity [SRC-039 Thales/Imperva] ⚠. That these products exist as polished commercial offerings is itself evidence that the cost of entry to large-scale automation has collapsed — though it says nothing about how often they are used for abuse versus legitimate data collection, a distinction the vendors are careful to draw and the project should not blur.

What this section deliberately does **not** cover: who profits from abuse, how proceeds are laundered or moved, or the structure of the criminal markets behind it. That is the out-of-scope "criminal economics" boundary noted above.

## The commercial defender landscape

The defence side has no OWASP. There is no neutral, public ontology of *defences* comparable to OAT's ontology of threats — the field is described almost entirely in the language of the vendors who sell it. This opacity is not incidental: as the project's framing puts it, part of the commercial moat *is* the opacity. Reading the landscape therefore means reading marketing as evidence and being disciplined about what it can and cannot establish.

The landscape sorts into rough categories — used here to organise the evidence, not to compare offerings:

- **Edge / CDN bot management**, where bot detection rides on an existing content-delivery position [SRC-002, SRC-003 Cloudflare, `capability-doc`]. The register now carries a much fuller Cloudflare picture — its layered detection-engine taxonomy, Detection IDs / coherence checks, Turnstile challenge model, the defensive-stack overview, and AI-crawler blocking [SRC-054, SRC-055, SRC-056, SRC-057, SRC-058, `capability-doc`/`methods-taxonomy`]. The value of this evidence is unchanged: it shows what signals a major control plane *exposes and uses* — bot scores, JA3/JA4 fingerprints, detection identifiers, rule fields — not whether any of them works in production.
- **Dedicated bot-management vendors** [SRC-004 DataDome, SRC-005/006/007 Netacea, SRC-008 Arkose, SRC-009 Kasada, SRC-010 HUMAN/PerimeterX]. Collectively these articulate the field's working vocabulary: intent- and journey-based detection, behavioural signals, scoring, dynamic challenges, and proof-of-execution. A scarce-resource-specific defence taxonomy is now also represented [SRC-049 DataDome ticket bots]. Each of these is a *claim about approach*, traceable to a source, and none is independently verified here.
- **WAF / broader application security** (F5, Imperva, Akamai) — previously flagged as a gap, now **partially extracted**: an F5 credential-stuffing report [SRC-034], the Thales/Imperva 2026 Bad Bot Report [SRC-039], and Akamai financial-services attack trends [SRC-040], all `empirical-operational` but vendor-measured ⚠. Note the asymmetry: Imperva is seen through its own report (SRC-039) and through a scraper-side bypass writeup [SRC-031], not through any neutral source.
- **Simple / self-managed controls** — the low end of the stack the enterprise-vendor framing tends to omit: ModSecurity/WAF rule packs and user-agent blocklists [SRC-051 StopBadBots] and an open ASN/datacenter blocklist for signup abuse [SRC-052 Hamachek, observed first-party anecdotal]. These are maintainer claims attached to tooling, not evaluated controls, and SRC-052 is dated and single-operator.
- **Anti-fraud adjacency** (Sift, Forter, Riskified, where bot-relevant) — still named in scope but **not yet in the register** ⚠. This remains a genuine gap.

A recurring discipline applies to the **quantitative** claims in this category: they are almost all vendor-measured and self-reported, and the page marks them rather than repeating them as fact.

- Netacea's executive survey reports a business-impact figure of roughly \$85.6M per company [SRC-007, evidence basis: `survey`] ⚠ — this is a 440-respondent self-reported survey, not a measured prevalence or cost.
- DataDome's "fully protected" exposure figure (on the order of a few percent of sites) [SRC-004, `vendor-claim`] ⚠ — vendor-measured against the vendor's own criteria, not independently reproducible.

When the first draft was written, the FP-Inconsistent work [SRC-015, evidence basis: `empirical-operational`] was described as the *single* external, operational check on a commercial detector. That is no longer accurate, and the judgement is updated here rather than silently: the register now holds several independent or observed-use anchors. FP-Inconsistent still ran purchased evasive bot traffic against commercial detection on a honey site and remains the strongest non-vendor evidence about a deployed *bot detector*, with the limit that its threat model is impression fraud on one honey site. Alongside it now sit an independent measurement of AI-agent detectability against a Cloudflare free-tier check [SRC-033 FP-Agent, measured (honey-site)], a leaked-credential honey-identity experiment [SRC-048 Wardle, measured], and a TLS/JA4 classification study whose headline AUC ≈ 0.998 is caveated by weak ("bot" in the app field) labels [SRC-018, measured (weak labels)]. The honest summary across all of these: independent operational evidence exists but is still **scarce and narrow** — each anchor is a single setting, a specific threat model, or a dated/weak-label dataset — and everything stronger than "the vendor claims" leans on this small set.

This is the concrete form of the corpus-wide pattern flagged at the top: per the register's `operational proximity` axis, the defender landscape concentrates at `capability` and `claimed`; `observed` is vendor-measured telemetry (SRC-004, SRC-009, SRC-010, SRC-034, SRC-036, SRC-038, SRC-039, SRC-040); and `measured` is essentially the handful of honey-site/weak-label studies above. The register evidences what the field *can do* and *says it sees* far more strongly than what actually happens in production.

## The AI-agent shift

The older bot-detection framing assumed a clean line between "human in a real browser" and "script pretending to be one." Browser-native automation erodes that line. Cloud-browser infrastructure runs automation inside real, instrumented browsers; browser extensions and userscripts operate *inside* otherwise-legitimate human sessions; and AI browser agents act on a user's behalf in ways that are neither classically human nor classically bot [SRC-026 cloud-browser/agent docs; SRC-008, SRC-009, SRC-010 on the defender-side reaction].

This is the part of the territory that is genuinely moving, and the register has expanded most here since the first draft. The defender-vendor view is now substantial: HUMAN's account of exposed autonomous-agent gateways producing browser-automation traffic [SRC-036 OpenClaw, observed], its agentic-visibility/trust-classification model [SRC-037], and a monthly telemetry snapshot of agentic traffic by sector and route [SRC-038 State of Agentic Traffic, observed]; Thales/Imperva framing the same shift as the "agentic age" [SRC-039]; Akamai on AI-labelled botnets and API-visibility gaps in financial services [SRC-040]; and Cloudflare's productisation of AI-crawler blocking [SRC-054]. All of this is vendor-visible traffic with opaque classification, and — importantly — agentic traffic is **not** the same as malicious traffic, a caveat the sources themselves make.

The first-draft claim that this surface is seen "almost entirely through defender-vendor framing… one side only" is now **partly outdated** and is softened here. Two non-vendor anchors exist: an independent academic measurement of AI-agent fingerprintability [SRC-033 FP-Agent], and a first-party operator account of AI-crawler pressure and residential-proxy evasion from a named platform [SRC-032 Wikimedia, observed first-party] — though the latter is an open-knowledge platform, not a commercial booking/e-commerce target, so its generality is limited. What remains genuinely missing is the agent-*builder* primary sources — Anthropic's Claude-in-Chrome safety material, OpenAI's agent material — which are named in scope but **still not extracted** ⚠. So the evidence is no longer one-sided between *defenders* and *independents*; it is still one-sided in that the people *building* the agents have not been read. That is now the most important specific gap in the section.

## What this section does not cover

By design: named threat actors and campaigns; the criminal revenue/money-flow economics; specific high-profile incidents; vendor comparison or ranking; the *mechanism* of individual detection techniques (that is Techniques); and the buildable-vs-must-buy boundary (that is the boundaries section). The WAF/appsec category is now partially covered (F5, Imperva/Thales, Akamai) but the anti-fraud vendor category (Sift, Forter, Riskified) and the agent-*builder* side of the AI-agent shift remain unextracted — these are evidence gaps, not deliberate exclusions, and are tracked below. The register has also picked up a privacy/regulatory strand (GDPR/AI Act review [SRC-047], scraping-legality explainer [SRC-042], the BOTS Act enforcement record [SRC-050]); whether that belongs on this page as territory description or sits outside scope as policy/advocacy is left open below rather than resolved, because the governing scope docs are not in front of me.

## Open questions

- Where exactly the in-scope/out-of-scope line on "economics" should sit — the cost-of-automation vs money-flow split taken above is a working judgement.
- Whether the defence-side landscape can be given any neutral structure at all, or whether it is irreducibly vendor-described — and if the latter, whether that opacity is itself a finding worth foregrounding.
- Whether the corpus-wide `operational proximity` finding — capability and market existence are well-evidenced; real-world prevalence and efficacy are not — should be the *headline* claim of this section rather than a caveat threaded through it.
- How much weight the small set of operational/measured anchors (SRC-015, SRC-033, SRC-048, SRC-018) can bear, given each is a single setting, a specific threat model, or a dated/weak-label dataset.
- Whether **scarce-resource abuse** now warrants its own subsection (or page), given it has moved from abstract OAT mapping to observed (SRC-035) and legal-record (SRC-050) evidence with a direct booking-style-worked-example fit.
- Whether the privacy/regulatory strand (SRC-047, SRC-042, SRC-050) belongs here as territory description or is out of scope as policy.

## Review-pending dependency ledger

What each section needs verified before publish. Until these are done, treat the section as raw material.

| Section | Leans on | Verify before publish |
|---|---|---|
| Threat model | SRC-001, SRC-027; SRC-035, SRC-050, SRC-039, SRC-034, SRC-048 | Review SRC-027 (`needs review`, extracted without repo scope docs) before treating any OAT code as confirmed; confirm the unnumbered codes (carding/card-cracking, loyalty/promotion, API/business-logic). Check SRC-035/SRC-050 are cited as platform/enforcement reports, not prevalence; verify the SRC-039 carding and SRC-034/SRC-048 credential claims against entries. |
| Adversary | SRC-011, SRC-012, SRC-047, SRC-033; SRC-021–026, SRC-029, SRC-045, SRC-046, SRC-053 | Confirm the sophistication-gradient definitions and advanced-bot AUC ≈ 0.68 against entries; confirm SRC-047 review-paper framing; confirm SRC-033 is read as benign-task/closed-world, not prevalence. Tooling-tier and bypass-mental-model sources (SRC-045/046) are high dual-use — confirm technique-family-only citation. |
| Economics | SRC-008, SRC-009, SRC-024, SRC-025, SRC-053, SRC-044, SRC-046, SRC-039 | Kasada attacker-cost pricing is vendor-reported — confirm figures against entry or present without numbers. Re-check the scope reconciliation against `EVIDENCE-REVIEW.md` §3. SRC-039 proxy/solver claims are vendor-measured. |
| Defender landscape | SRC-002–010, SRC-015, SRC-033, SRC-048, SRC-018, SRC-034, SRC-039, SRC-040, SRC-049, SRC-051, SRC-052, SRC-054–058 | Verify the Netacea \$85.6M survey and DataDome exposure figures against entries. Confirm the operational-proximity assignments (provisional, per register update log) before relying on the capability-vs-prevalence synthesis. SRC-039 scarce-resource fields are flagged review-before-use in the register. Anti-fraud (Sift/Forter/Riskified) still absent. |
| AI-agent shift | SRC-026, SRC-032, SRC-033, SRC-036, SRC-037, SRC-038, SRC-039, SRC-040, SRC-054 | Confirm SRC-036/038 are cited as vendor-observed with attribution caveats and that agentic ≠ malicious is preserved. Extract agent-builder primary sources (Anthropic, OpenAI) — still the key missing side. |
